result.question:
Research question: What techniques, tools, and recent developments (published up to 2026-07-16) can make the colibri engine faster WITHOUT losing output quality, on our specific setup?

Context (our system, already measured): colibri (github.com/JustVugg/colibri) is a pure-C engine running GLM-5.2 (744B-parameter MoE from Zhipu, int4 conversion, ~370 GB on disk) on consumer hardware by keeping the dense part (~10 GB) resident in RAM and streaming routed experts from NVMe per token through a per-layer LRU cache. Our hardware: Windows 11 laptop, 32 GB RAM, RTX 5060 8 GB VRAM, NVMe SSD. Current performance: native MTP (multi-token-prediction head) speculative decoding with 60% first-draft-token acceptance = 2.0 tok/forward; ~13 s per 744B forward pass (bottleneck is expert loading from disk across 78 layers, NOT drafting); warm multi-turn TTFT ~33 s; end-to-end ~0.03–0.15 tok/s. Already tried and REJECTED: external GPU draft model via shared-memory IPC (GLM-4 on the RTX, 45.5% acceptance — loses to native MTP's 60%), and Chinese-output compression (costs MORE tokens on this tokenizer). Engine env knobs we know: DIRECT, PILOT/PIPE (expert prefetch+overlap), CTX, KVSAVE, CACHE_ROUTE (opt-in expert-pinning routing that gave 2.4→3.33 tok/s on a GB10 machine per repo commit 62419af).

Research angles (cover all):
1. The colibri repo itself: recent commits, PRs, issues, discussions (especially #199 CACHE_ROUTE, #207), and any forks with speed work — what upstream features exist that we haven't turned on, and what do maintainer benchmarks say?
2. Speculative decoding advances 2025–2026 that are LOSSLESS (or measurably quality-neutral) and could beat or stack with plain MTP: EAGLE-2/3, dynamic/adaptive speculation depth, tree/beam speculation, self-speculation, multi-draft verification — and which of these are even applicable when the verifier is a CPU-bound disk-streamed MoE where verification (not drafting) is the bottleneck.
3. Competing/adjacent MoE-offloading engines to steal ideas from: KTransformers, PowerInfer/PowerInfer-2, MoE-Infinity, llama.cpp's MoE CPU-offload developments (--n-cpu-moe etc.), DeepSeek-V3/R1-style MTP implementations, academic work on expert prediction/prefetching and expert-cache locality (2025-2026).
4. Faster expert streaming on Windows specifically: DirectStorage for CPU workloads, unbuffered/overlapped I/O, io_uring-equivalents, NVMe queue-depth tuning, filesystem/mmap tricks, RAM-disk hybrids for hot experts given 32 GB total.
5. Community practice: Reddit r/LocalLLaMA, Hacker News (colibri hit 453 points July 2026), GitHub discussions on running GLM-5.2/GLM-4.5-class MoE locally — practical speedups people measured, config recipes, quantization variants that trade zero/negligible quality.
6. Anything brand-new (first half of 2026) we might not know: new inference engines, new GLM releases with better MTP heads, new Windows/NVIDIA features relevant to disk-streamed inference on 8 GB VRAM.

Deliverable: ranked list of concrete, implementable-on-our-setup optimizations (expected speedup, effort, quality risk, source links), separating (a) turn-on-now engine features, (b) implementable code changes, (c) promising-but-research-grade ideas. Language of the report: Romanian is fine for prose, keep technical terms in English.
result.summary: Could not verify any claims — all 25 verifier panels failed (likely rate-limiting or API errors). This is an infrastructure failure, not a research finding. Raw extracted claims returned below; retry or verify manually.

unverified.claim: Colibri's PILOT env knob enables router-lookahead disk prefetch: the next layer's expert routing is ~71.6% predictable from the current layer's post-attention state, and a dedicated I/O thread prefetches those experts while the current layer computes — directly targeting the expert-loading-from-disk bottleneck the user measured.

A 2026-07-10 upstream change fixed expert-cache auto-sizing so the engine now raises the LRU cap to fill the --ram budget instead of only lowering it; before this fix, large-RAM machines used the same cache size as 16 GB systems — a turn-on-now (pull-and-rebuild) win for the user's 32 GB machine if their build predates it.

The engine auto-learns hot experts across sessions: it records actual routing frequencies to a .coli_usage file and at startup automatically pins the hottest experts in spare RAM (controlled via PIN/PIN_GB/AUTOPIN/REPIN knobs); community benchmarks attribute measurable hit-rate gains to this (e.g., Framework 13 reaching 66% expert hit with auto-learned pinning).

MTP speculative decoding only works with an int8-quantized MTP head — the original model's int4 heads yield ~0% draft acceptance with silent failure, while a correctly configured int8 head gives 39-59% acceptance; additionally, on a cold cache speculation increases expert loads from ~660 to ~1100 per token and can be a net time loss until the cache warms, and speculation is lossless in exact arithmetic but not byte-identical to non-speculative greedy in practice.

In colibri's MTP speculative decoding on GLM-5.2, the positions of a verify batch route to mostly different experts, so expert compute time scales near-linearly with speculation batch size (80ms at S=1, 168ms at S=2, 306ms at S=4) — meaning deeper speculation multiplies expert work rather than amortizing it.

On the full-residency 6x5090 setup, MTP speculation reduced net throughput at every draft depth tested despite high acceptance rates (draft=1: 79% acceptance but 6.79→6.45 tok/s, -5%; draft=2: 64% acceptance, -17%; draft=3: 69% acceptance, -10%), and the maintainer's recommended setting is DRAFT=0 when experts are fully resident.

Speculative (MTP) verification in colibri can silently lose acceptance rate because the verifier does not reproduce the drafter's routing state; three independent bug reports share this root cause, with measured MTP acceptance collapses from 33% to 4% and from 38% to 21%, and a proposed fix is enforcing state-independent expert routing within draft/verify pairs.

Int2 quantization of streamed experts (which would roughly halve the disk bytes per token, the user's stated bottleneck) is not viable naively — naive int2 conversion produces ~45–60% error — and requires an incoherence-rotation-aware converter pass plus A/B quality testing to be quality-neutral (tracked as issue #81).

Quantizing the GLM-5.2 MTP (multi-token-prediction) heads to int4 degrades speculative-decoding acceptance so badly that they are practically unusable; keeping the heads at int8 restores usable acceptance.

Int8 MTP heads enable native speculative decoding in colibri and deliver a major overall inference speedup versus running without a working MTP head — though the card gives no numbers (no tok/s, no acceptance %, no perplexity).

A 4-bit quantized copy of an MoE model predicts the full-precision model's top-4 expert routing decisions with ~90.9% accuracy, so a cheap quantized draft pass can serve as an expert-prefetch oracle — directly relevant to colibri, where expert loading from NVMe (not drafting) is the bottleneck and predicted experts could be prefetched during the previous token's I/O window.

MoE-SpeQ's speculative expert prefetching (Expert Lookahead Buffer + hierarchical entropy-aware caching in three phases) achieves expert-cache hit rates above 96% under 16-32 GB memory budgets — i.e., proactive prediction-driven prefetch nearly eliminates on-demand expert fetches, versus a reactive per-layer LRU cache like colibri's.

The method is lossless in output quality: only the draft is INT4 (with non-expert parameters and shared experts kept in FP16), while every emitted token is verified by a single forward pass of the full-precision target model — the same verify-with-target guarantee colibri's native MTP already relies on, so the technique stacks without quality risk.

Speculative decoding can be used specifically to hide MoE expert-offloading latency, and the SpecMoEOff system achieves up to 2.5x decode throughput over state-of-the-art MoE offloading techniques by enlarging the expert workload per transfer.

The core mechanism is that verifying multiple draft tokens per target forward pass amortizes expert transfer cost, because the increased per-transfer workload matches the underutilized-GPU regime of MoE offloading — the same regime as colibri, where expert loading (not drafting) dominates each forward pass.

Tree-based speculative decoding's advantage weakens on sparse MoE models because different draft branches activate different experts, so the union of activated experts grows with the draft tree and target-side verification cost rises substantially — directly analogous to colibri, where each extra activated expert is an extra NVMe load during the 744B verification pass.

EVICT is a training-free, hyperparameter-free, and LOSSLESS adaptive verification method that truncates the draft tree before target verification, keeping only the cost-effective prefix (estimated acceptance benefit vs offline-profiled verification cost) — an implementable idea for colibri: budget MTP speculation depth by expected expert-load cost rather than fixed depth.

EVICT cuts the number of activated experts during verification substantially (the paper body reports a 32.5% average reduction) while sacrificing only a modest fraction of mean accepted tokens (e.g., 3.09 vs EAGLE-3's 3.65 on Qwen3-30B-A3B) — i.e., in MoE spec decoding, trading a little acceptance length for far fewer expert activations is net-positive, which for colibri translates to fewer disk reads per accepted token.

A subset of a MoE model's own routed experts can serve as the draft model for speculative decoding (self-speculation), generating draft tokens with fewer experts that are then verified by the full expert routing — no separate draft network needed.
Under adaptive verification, SS-MoE achieves a 3.72x decoding speedup over state-of-the-art expert-offloading methods, but accuracy is only 'nearly lossless' (not strictly lossless) in that mode.

SS-MoE's conservative verification mode preserves model accuracy exactly while still decoding faster than the 4-bit quantized version of the same model — i.e., a strictly quality-preserving configuration exists.

EntMTP is a training-free inference-time scheduler that dynamically switches the speculative-decoding tree topology (and thus speculation depth) based on a running estimate of local generation entropy, instead of the static tree used by existing MTP-head models.

EntMTP is a lossless acceleration method: it does not fine-tune the base model or relax the standard acceptance condition, and measured continuation perplexity stays within 0.022 nats of the Hydra baseline.

In MoE models, speculative decoding verification with draft trees activates far more unique experts per layer than autoregressive decoding, and this expert-loading blow-up (memory/bandwidth pressure) is what erodes speculative-decoding speedups — e.g. a 127-token draft tree activates 54 of 64 experts per layer on OLMoE-1B-7B. This directly matches colibri's measured situation where verification (expert loading from NVMe), not drafting, is the bottleneck.

MoE-Spec is a training-free, verification-time technique: it enforces a fixed expert capacity budget B per layer by aggregating routing probabilities across all drafted tokens and loading only the top-B experts, with dropped experts handled by truncation (zero contribution) or substitution (rerouting to the highest-scoring available experts). Being training-free and verification-side, it is in principle implementable in colibri's C expert-streaming path.
